					<description><![CDATA[<p>Optical methods need to comply with increasing warpage, finer RDL pitch, and trace size.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/inspection-and-metrology-catching-up-for-high-density-fan-out-panel-packaging/">Inspection And Metrology Catching Up For High-Density Fan-Out Panel Packaging</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p><strong>Key Takeaways: </strong></p>
<ul>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">To support AI/HPC devices, high-density fan-out on panels must deliver increased RDL layer count and micropillar height while decreasing the trace and bump/micropillar pitch. </span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Metrology and inspection steps assist with achieving known-good panel requirements to avoid throwing away expensive chiplets, such as HBM and TPUs.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Optical measurement systems need to account for panel warpage.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></li>
</ul>
<hr />
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Soaring AI/HPC device demand is driving leading-edge foundries to support the transition from wafers to panels to accommodate increasingly larger device sizes. But to ensure that panels with multiple redistribution layers are known-good, inspection equipment must cope with shrinking RDL features, smaller bump pitch, and the warpage that comes with panels’ larger form factors.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Once primarily relegated to small chips, panel-level packaging has become increasingly attractive for AI/HPC device makers as package size grows to 80 x 80mm and beyond. While the thermal management associated with high energy consumption has been a prevailing concern, the insatiable need for more compute power in a single package has reached an area limit. Silicon interposer-based packages are generally limited to 100 x 100mm, the largest size at which yield, lithography, warpage, and cost are reasonable. But with panel-level packaging, form factors will range from 310 x 310 mm to 600&#215;600 mm, which TSMC has said will complement CoWoS, not replace it, in some very large packages.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">However, meeting the interconnect density requirements of high-density fan-out (HDFO) pushes panel manufacturers into new territory.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“It represents a challenge for PLP manufacturers due to large package sizes, the integration of multiple chiplets or devices in the same package, and more interconnect density enabled by lower pitches, smaller RDL L/S, and more RDL layer count,” said Gabriela Pereira, senior technology and market analyst for semiconductor packaging at Yole Group.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Fan-out panel-level packaging typically uses a chip-last manufacturing process; hence, the need for inspection to assure known-good panels. To support HDFO, layer count goes from 3 to 9, and trace and bump pitches decrease from 20 to 5 microns. In addition, several industry experts said that manufacturers are targeting 2 micron pitches within the next 5 years.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“For panel-level packaging, the first thing is that the manufacturing requirements have become more stringent. The smallest feature size becomes more like the front-end (i.e., wafer processing). This drives more inspection,” said Monita Pau, senior director of product marketing at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/onto-innovation/">Onto Innovation</a><span data-contrast="auto">. “If you look at panel level packaging you&#8217;re committing known good dies onto a package, in either an interposer or a substrate. You better make sure that these are defect-free before you commit a very expensive KGD onto the package.” </span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Engineering teams rely upon metrology, inspection, and electrical test to develop and maintain a panel manufacturing process with sufficient yield and quality. Early and frequent measurement provides the data to detect process drift, process anomalies, and defects. That, in turn, enables tweaks to process development and material properties, as well as prompt response to yield and quality excursions during production. </span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">On the positive side, engineers have a broad set of measurement tools at their disposal. Optical inspection remains the primary method, but non-optical solutions may soon be needed.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Existing advanced packaging metrology and inspection tool platforms are largely built around existing optical inspection and optical metrology platforms, adapted for panel formats,” said Yole’s Pereira. “The main techniques in use are optical AOI, optical 2D/3D, confocal, interferometry, profiler-based metrology, warpage and bow metrology tools, overlay and CD optical inspection, visual and optical carrier inspection for glass panels or glass carriers. Leading suppliers such as KLA and Onto Innovation leverage cross-platform architectures that can be adapted to PLP and advanced IC substrates with minimal modifications, while some vendors have developed PLP-dedicated tools.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Still, the shift to HDFO panels does demand more from the inspection and metrology equipment.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“This migration brings wafer/fab class requirements onto formats that were historically inspected with PCB-grade sensitivity,” said Arun Aiyer, CEO and CTO of Avarustech. “These requirements include sub-2µm RDL line/space, sub-20µm bump pitch, sub-micron overlay, hybrid-bond-ready surfaces, transparent-substrate handling, and through-glass via (TGV) characterization. At the same time, panel-size standardization, warpage control, and equipment readiness remain unresolved. These changes tighten process windows and amplify mechanical distortion. Inspection and metrology must therefore move from a discrete quality gate to a core enabler of manufacturability.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Measuring x,y and z for RDL and bumps</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">Multiple industry experts note that achieving high yield remains difficult for HDFO panels. Metrology and inspection provide essential data to yield and quality engineers. To support AI/HPC device designs, the layer count increases, and trace pitch and bump/pillar pitch decrease, but micropillar height increases. Inspection and metrology steps must be added at every layer, and the measurement resolution must increase with decreased feature size.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Miniaturization stresses the optical systems for both resolution and the ensuing throughput time per panel. </span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“When you have smaller objects, your optics resolution should also get smaller than the size of the object you are going to inspect. Currently, the minimum RDL line width is 5 microns in mass production, with future projections to shrink to 2 microns,” said JD Shin, director of customer success at Koh Young Technology. “For easier calculation, let&#8217;s choose the current line-width of 5 micrometers and a future line width of 1 micrometers. This means your resolution needs to be 5 times smaller than the conventional resolution. If you halve the resolution with the same type of camera, your inspection speed will be slower by four times.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">This increase in throughput time directly corresponds to the camera’s field of view, pixel count, and the requirement for improved resolution. The tradeoff between measurement sensitivity and the speed of measurement is typically addressed with faster physical scanning and the associated calculations. But it’s also a data management challenge. To put this in perspective, consider that the inspection of a 600 mm panel with 1 micron resolution results in ~10</span><span data-contrast="auto">12</span><span data-contrast="auto"> pixels per layer. </span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“It&#8217;s always sensitivity versus throughput,” said Onto’s Pau. “Either you have high sensitivity and lower throughput, or you have high throughput but lower sensitivity. Usually you try to balance these two aspects, because you want to make sure you are not missing killer defects. But on the other hand, you don&#8217;t want to go super slow.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Vias connect the RDL layers. As the number of layers increases, the via stack-up grows, but the via area decreases. The mechanical stresses inherent in material properties, smaller vias, and the manufacturing process affect via formation. AOI is limited in evaluating the via quality.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“The RDL layers are becoming complicated from 3 layers up to 9 layers,&#8221; said Kyoung Rok Park, corporate vice president of research and development at Amkor Technology. &#8220;That has an impact on the stacking, i.e., the vias between layers. Even if we perform inspection at every single layer with AOI, how do you know about the stacking?”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Park noted that today electrical test is used to screen via defects. However, earlier detection methods provide more timely data for engineers to correct processing, as well as screening for known-good panels. That, in turn, opens the door for other modalities such as infrared and X-ray. </span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">As bumps/micropillars’ pitch decreases and aspect ratio increases, traditional 2D inspection becomes insufficient because bump/micropillar quality affects the bonding yield and quality. With multiple camera angles, optical measurement equipment can create 3D images, and the resulting richer data can provide additional insight.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Three-dimensional measurement/inspection becomes more critical than in the past when you had bigger bump sizes,” noted Koh Young’s Shin. “If you have smaller sizes, making the perfect bump of the same size is much more challenging. That is the reason why inspection is needed in 3D. Our equipment can provide this type of measurement, including the bump height and volume.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-large wp-image-24278467" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Image-1.jpeg?resize=1024%2C371&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1024" height="371" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Image-1.jpeg?resize=1024%2C371&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Image-1.jpeg?resize=300%2C109&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Image-1.jpeg?resize=768%2C278&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Image-1.jpeg?resize=600%2C217&amp;ssl=1 600w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Image-1.jpeg?w=1407&amp;ssl=1 1407w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /><br />
<strong>Fig. 1: 3D measurement of solder bump. Source: Koy Young Technology </strong></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">In the bumps/micropillar lithography process, photoresist thickness is an important parameter. Before the panel is exposed, irregularities in the photoresist can be detected and reworked through removal and redeposition. But that requires measuring thick films (hundreds of microns) at nanometer resolution, and this combination of specs is a challenge for traditional optical measurement techniques.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“You lose yield if bumps or micropillars are not coplanar. As the 3D stacking gets higher, and as the dimension of the die gets larger, you&#8217;re putting more components on the micropillars. Yet as the pillar height gets taller your planarity requirements remain the same. This makes it more difficult to manufacture,” said Chris Claypool, senior director of R&amp;D for FilmTek product line at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/bruker/">Bruker</a><span data-contrast="auto">. “There are metrology tools that measure the coplanarity after the bumps and pillars are formed. We measure further up the line in the lithography stage, where they&#8217;re measuring the resist thickness and the resist uniformity. Because you can reprocess at that step. Normally with ellipsometry or reflectometry, you&#8217;re not going to be able to measure anything thicker than 30 microns.&#8221;</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Bruker has an optical design that enables measuring up to 2,000 microns of dielectric thickness. &#8220;That&#8217;s unnecessary for front-end applications, but in packaging, particularly for bump and pillar planarity, it’s critical to have nanometer resolution for very thick films,&#8221; Claypool said. &#8220;What we&#8217;re seeing is in the packaging the thicknesses originally were in the 50 to 100 micron range for the bumps. Now the last generation was up to 450 microns, and the next generation we&#8217;re working on now is going to be up to 650 microns.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Coping with warpage</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">As panel area increases engineers worry about the effect that warpage has on manufacturing processes. This includes the metrology and inspection methods which provide vital feedback on the RDL trace pitch, contamination defects, missing or excess metal on vias or micropillars, etc. Thus, optical measurement systems, the primary spectrum of choice, need to deliver good-quality measurements despite warpage. </span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“The biggest challenges are concentrated around scale, flatness, alignment, and process variability,&#8221; said Yole&#8217;s Pereira. &#8220;Process uniformity becomes harder because temperature, pressure, and material properties can vary across a much larger area. Warpage control is one of the most critical challenges, creating handling instability, die shift, photolithography alignment errors, yield loss, reliability issues, and electrical performance degradation. Hence, there is a need to adapt existing AOI, optical metrology, warpage metrology, profiler, interferometry, and 3D inspection platforms to larger panels. Equipment suppliers are scaling field of view, stage handling, data processing, warpage measurement, and automation so that wafer-level methods can work on larger panel formats.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">For optical measurement systems, the field of view and focal depth influence resolution and throughput. Warpage can affect optical focus, and consequently the measurement.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Another challenge is that if you have finer resolution, from optical point of perspective the focal depth becomes more critical,&#8221; said Shin. &#8220;If you have small resolution, you have much narrower focal depths. So even with very small warpage, the image that you receive can be very blurry.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Handling of panels can mitigate warpage.</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">“One of the biggest challenges with panel-level packaging is that you&#8217;re dealing with a much larger size than a wafer. The handling differs from a wafer form factor,” said Onto’s Pau. “Even though we do wafer-level packaging, you can imagine the panel surface area is much bigger. It&#8217;s always struggling with warpage. How are you going to hold the panel so that you can still do inspection?”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Others also stress the requirements on panel handling and focus capability that warpage mitigation requires.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“The first challenge you face is the warpage. How do you handle the warped panel, i.e., how do you suck it down and make it flatter? Because if you want the throughput, you need your system to quickly travel from one point to another,” said Samuel Lesko, senior director general manager of TSOM Business Unit at Bruker. “But with optical instruments, you have a certain working distance for focus. So you have to be careful to flatten out a full panel and avoid any collision with the instrumentation. The second challenge is with throughput. You need to have a reference base, such that the auto-focus time is minimal. So either you find a way to suck down the panel, or you find a way to get fast auto-focus. Or, you combine both techniques. And this choice is also a function of the panel/substrate material.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">However, the increased manufacturing process complexity results in a set of defects that are not visible to optics.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“The central challenge is that panel packaging combines wafer-class feature sizes with PCB-class mechanical instability. This stresses resolution, throughput, and stability in ways that existing tools were not designed to handle. Large panels deform, warp, shrink, expand, and locally shift far less predictably than wafers,” observed Avarustech’s Aiyer. “FOPLP (fan-out panel-level packaging) shows significantly higher warpage than equivalent FOWLP (fan-out wafer-level packaging) because CTE-induced warpage scales with dimension. Differential thermal expansion between polymers, metals, and silicon dies can induce severe bow and die shift during curing and debonding. As a result, critical defects are no longer purely visual. They are coupled to substrate distortion, residual stress, RDL deformation, coplanarity, and buried-interface behavior. A panel can pass conventional AOI and still fail because local height, overlay, or stress signatures were not measured in context.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Conclusion</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">AI/HPC devices are expected to shift to HDFO panel-level packaging. But the transition is not as simple as adopting wafer-level techniques known to achieve high-density RDL over to a panel substrate. As RDL layers increase and pitch and size decrease, inspection and metrology become even more important in factories than ever before.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Optical inspection and metrology equipment (AOI) continues to be the most common wavelength used by engineers for RDL panel manufacturing. But the combination of specs and panel form-factor challenges its detection ability due to mechanical instability that accompanies panel materials and size. In other words, warpage complicates every aspect of optical measurement techniques.</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Panel-level packaging inspection and metrology is at an inflection point. Legacy PCB and substrate AOI are being pushed to their limits. At the same time, semiconductor-grade metrology has only been partially adapted to the scale, materials, and mechanical instability of large rectangular substrates up to roughly 600 × 600mm,” noted Avarushtech’s Aiyer. “The major shift is that inspection is no longer only a pass/fail step. It is becoming the data engine for adaptive lithography, where offline die-shift metrology feeds ML algorithms that drive global, zonal, per-die, or site-by-site overlay correction. Still, the industry lacks a single high-throughput, closed-loop, panel-native platform that can correlate visual defects, dimensional errors, and process-induced deformation in one coordinate frame across the full panel.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Related Articles</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"> </span><br />
<a href="https://semiengineering.com/panel-level-packagings-second-wave-meets-engineering-reality/"><span data-contrast="none">Panel-Level Packaging’s Second Wave Meets Engineering Reality</span></a><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"><br />
The cost case is strengthening, but glass, warpage, and bonding yield stand in the way.</span></p>
<p><a href="https://semiengineering.com/fan-out-panel-level-packaging-hurdles/"><span data-contrast="none">Fan-Out Panel-Level Packaging Hurdles</span></a><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"><br />
The economics look attractive, but first the industry needs convergence on panel size, process tools, and materials.</span></p>
<p><a href="https://semiengineering.com/whats-failing-at-the-interface/"><span data-contrast="none">What’s Failing At The Interface</span></a><span data-ccp-props="{&quot;201341983&quot;:0,&quot;335559739&quot;:0,&quot;335559740&quot;:240}"><br />
In advanced packages, the interface is where problems show up, but rarely where they begin.</span></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/inspection-and-metrology-catching-up-for-high-density-fan-out-panel-packaging/">Inspection And Metrology Catching Up For High-Density Fan-Out Panel Packaging</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>

		<item>
		<title>From Reactive Replacement To Predictive Planning: Unlocking Probe Card Intelligence With Real-Time Data</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/from-reactive-replacement-to-predictive-planning-unlocking-probe-card-intelligence-with-real-time-data/</link>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>DC profiling–based analytics could reduce downtime, protect yield, and improve operational stability across wafer test environments. </p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/from-reactive-replacement-to-predictive-planning-unlocking-probe-card-intelligence-with-real-time-data/">From Reactive Replacement To Predictive Planning: Unlocking Probe Card Intelligence With Real-Time Data</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Probe cards are among the most critical — and costly — assets in wafer test. Even if production line typically prepares on-demand spares, unexpected failure can cause significant downtime and production loss. One of the most persistent challenges is tip-burn degradation: gradual probe tips wear that ultimately leads to failure.</p>
<p>A new technical initiative explores whether DC profiling data can provide early warning — days or even weeks before a probe card fails — while also advancing a broader vision for real-time, intelligent semiconductor test operations.</p>
<p>Rather than focusing solely on failure detection, the initiative introduces a new operational model: bringing real-time streaming, analytics, and production feedback directly into the test loop.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278440" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig1.webp?resize=753%2C1038&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="753" height="1038" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig1.webp?w=753&amp;ssl=1 753w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig1.webp?resize=218%2C300&amp;ssl=1 218w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig1.webp?resize=743%2C1024&amp;ssl=1 743w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig1.webp?resize=600%2C827&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 753px) 100vw, 753px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 1: AI adding value in semiconductor test.</strong></p>
<h2>The core idea: Stability contains insight</h2>
<p>During wafer test, DC profiling captures currents and voltages that are expected to remain relatively stable under normal operating conditions. Subtle deviations — spikes, gradual drift, or emerging behavioral trends — may indicate early probe degradation long before catastrophic failure occurs.</p>
<p>Historically, these signals have existed but were difficult to access continuously or analyze in production timeframes. The opportunity now is to transform DC profiling from background measurement into a real-time intelligence signal.</p>
<p>The approach progresses through two phases: infrastructure enablement followed by predictive analytics — both enabled by real-time data streaming.</p>
<h2>Phase 1: Real-time DC profiling data collection</h2>
<p>The first phase focuses on establishing purpose-built real-time streaming for DC profiling data. This is not generic data collection. The objective is to extract DC profiling information directly from test programs using ACS Nexus infrastructure and make it continuously available through ACS Real-Time Data Infrastructure (ACS RTDI).</p>
<p>In this phase, ACS provides proven streaming infrastructure while customers retain flexibility to develop and deploy their own analytics models.</p>
<p>Key capabilities enabled include:</p>
<ul>
<li>Real-time streaming with inline analytics rather than offline data mining</li>
<li>Reduced manual data preparation and post-processing</li>
<li>Immediate accessibility to high-fidelity DC profiling signals</li>
<li>Secure, production-ready data pipelines</li>
</ul>
<p>By bringing RTDI streaming and RTDI applications into the operational loop, analytics can begin operating during production instead of after production.</p>
<p>This establishes the foundation for intelligent, responsive test environments.</p>
<h2>Phase 2: Anomaly detection and predictive analytics</h2>
<p>With continuous DC streams available, the next step introduces anomaly detection models designed to identify non-constant behavior associated with impending Tip-burn degradation or probe card failure.</p>
<p>Early experiments on limited datasets show encouraging signals:</p>
<ul>
<li>DC behavior is not perfectly constant over lifetime operation</li>
<li>Observable spikes and drift patterns may precede degradation</li>
<li>Trend analysis suggests measurable predictive signatures</li>
</ul>
<p>Challenges remain, including:</p>
<ul>
<li>Limited historical datasets</li>
<li>Managing false positives and false negatives</li>
<li>Validating predictive performance at production scale</li>
</ul>
<p>Access to larger production datasets — often held by fabless companies — will be essential for advancing predictive accuracy.</p>
<p>Importantly, RTDI streaming enables analytics to evolve continuously as more production data becomes available, allowing models to improve over time rather than remain static.</p>
<h2>Phase 3: Large language model RCA reasoning</h2>
<p>Once an anomaly is flagged by the NV-Tesseract model, the application gathers context from the tester, such as profiling data, junction temperature and SmarTest logs. This enriched data is then fed into a local LLM, the Nvidia Nemotron model, a highly efficient model designed for Agentic AI that runs inference on the ACS Edge server.</p>
<p>Nemotron reasons over the context + the tesseract output and lot/site/wafer statistics to reach a root cause analysis text, generating a RCA hypothesis and follow up migration actions.</p>
<p>The root cause analysis is computed in real time (available even before lot end), being a closed loop intelligence that significantly reduces the RCA time.</p>
<h2>Expanding beyond single-tool optimization</h2>
<p>A key advantage of RTDI-enabled analytics is the ability to learn across environments.</p>
<p>Traditional approaches such as tester matching or board-level monitoring typically operate within isolated systems. In contrast, real-time streaming allows learning across:</p>
<ul>
<li>Multiple testers</li>
<li>Different products</li>
<li>Entire manufacturing sites</li>
</ul>
<p>This broader visibility enables comparative learning that can reveal patterns invisible within single-tool analysis. Over time, insights gained in one environment can inform optimization elsewhere, creating a continuously improving platform.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278441" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig2.webp?resize=1024%2C1536&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1024" height="1536" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig2.webp?w=1024&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig2.webp?resize=200%2C300&amp;ssl=1 200w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig2.webp?resize=683%2C1024&amp;ssl=1 683w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig2.webp?resize=768%2C1152&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig2.webp?resize=600%2C900&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 2: Anomaly detection predicting probe card failures.</strong></p>
<h2>Enabling closed-loop production intelligence</h2>
<p>Beyond prediction, real-time infrastructure opens the door to closed-loop production workflows.</p>
<p>Analytics no longer serve only as reporting tools. Instead, insights can drive actions directly within manufacturing operations, including:</p>
<ul>
<li>Adjusting test conditions based on historical device behavior</li>
<li>Enabling adaptive decision-making during production</li>
<li>Supporting automated feedback mechanisms across test insertions</li>
</ul>
<p>This shift moves semiconductor test toward adaptive operations — where systems respond dynamically to real-time information.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278442" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig3.webp?resize=1280%2C608&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1280" height="608" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig3.webp?w=1280&amp;ssl=1 1280w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig3.webp?resize=300%2C143&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig3.webp?resize=1024%2C486&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig3.webp?resize=768%2C365&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig3.webp?resize=600%2C285&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 3: Anomaly detection app dashboard – real time analytics.</strong></p>
<h2>Low-latency analytics with secure data control</h2>
<p>Integrating analytics into production requires both performance and trust.</p>
<p>RTDI architecture supports low-latency data access and accelerated computing while maintaining strong data governance. Secure streaming, controlled access policies, and protected data handling help safeguard customer intellectual property while enabling collaboration and advanced analytics.</p>
<p>The result is a balance between speed and security — enabling innovation without compromising protection.</p>
<p><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone size-full wp-image-24278443" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig4.webp?resize=1276%2C510&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="1276" height="510" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig4.webp?w=1276&amp;ssl=1 1276w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig4.webp?resize=300%2C120&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig4.webp?resize=1024%2C409&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig4.webp?resize=768%2C307&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Advantest_Reactive-to-Predictive-Planning-Probe-Card-fig4.webp?resize=600%2C240&amp;ssl=1 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1000px) 100vw, 1000px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig. 4: Anomaly detection app dashboard – LLM root cause analysis and reasoning. </strong></p>
<h2>Looking ahead: A continuously improving platform</h2>
<p>This initiative remains early-stage but aligns with a broader industry transition toward intelligent semiconductor manufacturing.</p>
<p>Its long-term value extends beyond probe card prediction:</p>
<ul>
<li>Proven infrastructure enabling scalable real-time data access</li>
<li>Production-grade analytics operating directly within test flows</li>
<li>A continuously improving platform that learns across customers, products, and sites</li>
</ul>
<p>DC profiling–based analytics could shift probe card management from reactive replacement to predictive planning — reducing downtime, protecting yield, and improving operational stability across wafer test environments.</p>
<p>More broadly, it represents a step toward semiconductor test systems that learn continuously, adapt automatically, and transform data into operational foresight.</p>
<p>This strategy strongly supports Advantest’s recent announcement of leveraging advanced machine learning technology (both hardware and software) from NVIDIA with the <a href="https://www.advantest.com/en/products/acs/rtdi/">Advantest Cloud Solutions Real-Time Data Infrastructure</a> (ACS RTDI) to drive a shift from traditional test workflows to adaptive AI-driven systems. Some of the highlights of this multivariate ML-based Anomaly Detection solution include:</p>
<ol>
<li>The Anomaly Detection App uses Nvidia’s NV-Tesseract library to detect abnormal patterns across multiple, related timeseries signals (“waveforms”) by learning what normal behavior looks like and flagging deviations—at very high speed using Nvidia GPU acceleration.</li>
<li>The LLM RCA (Root Cause Analysis) loop, an application-level reasoning layer built on top of NVIDIA technologies like Nemotron, transforms anomaly detection from simple alerting into autonomous problem-solving by reasoning over system context, generating root cause explanations, and driving targeted mitigation actions in a closed feedback loop.</li>
<li>The NV-Tesseract enables early tip-burn anomaly detection by using multivariate data such as parametric test results and DC profiling waveform. GPU-accelerated time-series modeling makes it practical to detect subtle deviations early, while LLM-based reasoning layers can interpret these signals in an operational context, supporting predictive probe health assessment before yield-impacting failures can occur.</li>
</ol>
<p>Testing has long been the cornerstone of chip manufacturing, ensuring every device meets exacting standards of quality and performance. Traditionally, this required weeks of data collection, fault analysis, and test deployment cycles. ACS RTDI moves testing from validation to prediction—transforming semiconductor production into an AI-driven, continuously adaptive process.</p>
<p>Integrated with NVIDIA AI inference, ACS RTDI could bring real-time intelligence to semiconductor testing. Massive data streams are ingested through ACS Data-Feed-Forward cross-insertion, where GPU-accelerated compute optimizes the test set for every chip. This scalable GPU architecture expands seamlessly, supporting the concurrent training of multiple ML models—enabling non-stop operation to drive yield gains, dynamically optimized test coverage, and sharp reductions in latency, power, and cost.</p>
<p>ACS RTDI has demonstrated its robustness at high-volume production sites, securely supporting AI/ML-driven test automation across diverse applications. Its flexible architecture—separating data preparation, algorithms, and decisioning—empowers manufacturers to rapidly adapt as production needs evolve.</p>
<h2>Building the AI-driven test facility of the future</h2>
<p>Advantest also plans to incorporate <a href="http://www.nvidia.com/en-us/ai-data-science/products/nemo/">NVIDIA’s NeMo</a> and <a href="https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/ai-data-science/products/nim-microservices/">NVIDIA NIM microservices</a> into ACS semiconductor test analytics solutions. These technologies will curate heterogeneous production data, evaluate models, and deploy AI agents capable of running generative AI applications directly in the test environment.</p>
<p>Through this integration, Advantest is setting the stage for the next wave of semiconductor innovation—where AI not only accelerates the chip development process but also transforms how such chips are tested, validated, and delivered to market.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/from-reactive-replacement-to-predictive-planning-unlocking-probe-card-intelligence-with-real-time-data/">From Reactive Replacement To Predictive Planning: Unlocking Probe Card Intelligence With Real-Time Data</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>As margins shrink and dies move into expensive packages, separating device failures from test-cell artifacts has become a first-order economic problem. </p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/when-the-test-cell-lies/">When The Test Cell Lies</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Key Takeaways: </span></b><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<ul>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">A marginal test result can be electrically valid and still diagnostically misleading because the socket, load board, and thermal loop are now part of the measurement. </span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Separating device drift from test-cell drift depends on tracking margins, variance, and calibration trends rather than bins alone. </span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">In advanced packages, a false pass destroys value downstream, a false failure discards it early, and a false downgrade hides the loss inside normal binning.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></li>
</ul>
<hr />
<p><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span><span data-contrast="auto">The first problem with a marginal test result is that it looks like an answer. The second is deciding whether it belongs to the device or the test cell. </span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The device passes, fails, bins lower, or recovers on retest, and the test cell appears to have done its job. It produced a number. The trouble is that the number </span><span data-contrast="auto">may</span> <span data-contrast="auto">be accurate inside the narrow conditions in which it was taken while still pointing engineers toward the wrong conclusion about the device itself.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Everything in this conversation comes back to one question: </span><span data-contrast="auto">&#8216;D</span><span data-contrast="auto">o you trust the number?</span><span data-contrast="auto">&#8216;</span><span data-contrast="auto">” said </span><span data-contrast="auto">Ram Channappa</span><span data-contrast="auto">, senior business development leader </span><span data-contrast="auto">for</span><span data-contrast="auto"> AI Hyperscaler at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/keysight-technologies/">Keysight</a><span data-contrast="auto">. “You cannot separate real device drift from instrument drift if your instrument is the least stable thing in the room.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">It’s not just an instrumentation question</span><span data-contrast="auto">. Marginal test results may relate to the socket, probe card, load board, handler, thermal loop, calibration record, and the data infrastructure tying it all together. A failure may be real in the sense that the device missed the limit as applied, but that doesn’t prove the silicon was the weak point. A bad contact, a drifting channel, a hot probe card, or an unrepresentative workload can produce a result that is electrically valid but diagnostically misleading.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">That means test engineers are no longer just deciding whether a part passed. They must decide whether the measurement environment was stable enough, and representative enough, to make that verdict meaningful. The hard part is that a modern test cell can fail softly, degrading a margin, shifting a bin, or eroding confidence long before anything breaks outright.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“This is basically the pain of all test engineers</span><span data-contrast="auto"> — </span><span data-contrast="auto">h</span><span data-contrast="auto">ow to distinguish between a real failure and a false failure</span><span data-contrast="auto">,” said Fabio Pizza, test technology director at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/advantest-corporation/">Advantest</a><span data-contrast="auto">. “</span><span data-contrast="auto">It’s important to build a reliable database with data to compare whenever you have a failure.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Two failure paths diverged in the woods</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">The first useful question is usually not what failed</span><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-contrast="auto">It&#8217;s </span><span data-contrast="auto">what</span><span data-contrast="auto"> path the failure follows. If the same device fails across sites, boards, and testers, the device becomes the stronger suspect. If the failure </span><span data-contrast="auto">persists</span><span data-contrast="auto"> with a site, channel, socket, or handler position while different devices move through it, suspicion shifts to the test cell. But if the behavior tracks temperature, time, insertion count, or a wafer-map pattern, the story is already more complicated than pass/fail can capture.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“The clearest indicator that a failure is tester-side</span><span data-contrast="auto">,</span><span data-contrast="auto"> rather than device-side</span><span data-contrast="auto">,</span><span data-contrast="auto"> is the failure stays attached to the same instrument channel, slot, or test site even as different devices rotate through it,” said Chuck Carline, director of factory applications at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/teradyne-corporation/">Teradyne</a><span data-contrast="auto">. “In a properly operating system, the expectation is that yield will be similar across all sites, so any significant differences will indicate a potential issue with the test cell.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">That sounds straightforward until it </span><span data-contrast="auto">happens</span><span data-contrast="auto"> in a high-site-count production environment.</span><span data-contrast="auto"> </span><span data-contrast="auto">Every</span><span data-contrast="auto"> site adds another combination of instrument resources, load-board paths, contacts, power delivery, grounding, and local mechanical variation. Meanwhile, large AI and HPC die shrink the number of die per wafer, making it harder to build a clean statistical picture before yield decisions come due.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“At probe, regular patterns of failures can show up clearly in the wafer map,” said Carline. “The exact pattern will depend on the probe stepping pattern, but it is often apparent to the human eye.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The device is only one piece of the temporary system that makes the measurement possible. The contactor, board, channel, handler, and thermal hardware are all transient parts of the circuit, and each can add just enough variation to change the apparent result. That is why substitution remains such a durable diagnostic method, even in increasingly data-rich flows. Swap sockets or instruments across sites, and watch whether the error follows the hardware or stays with the location.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The logic is old, but the stakes are not. A setup problem once meant some yield loss, retest, and a debug cycle. </span><span data-contrast="auto">Now it </span><span data-contrast="auto">can m</span><span data-contrast="auto">ean scrapping or downgrading very expensive silicon, or worse, letting a marginal die move into a package where the cost of the escape is multiplied by everything attached to it. The test cell is no longer just the last checkpoint before shipment. It has become a place where the economics of advanced packaging either works or gets quietly compromised.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">The interface is part of the measurement</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">The socket, probe card, or contactor is often treated as supporting infrastructure, but electrically it’s part of the measurement. It has to deliver power, ground, and signals through a mechanical structure that wears, heats, and contaminates. As margins shrink, the difference between a good contact and a marginal one looks less like a maintenance issue and more like a product-quality issue.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Every test you run is dependent on that interconnect,” said Jack Lewis, director of applications and product management at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/modus-test/">Modus Test</a><span data-contrast="auto">. “It’s a mechanical system that’s being used as an electrical interconnect, and it’s got any number of mechanical defects that can occur from the actual physical wear, plating wearing off, springs getting weak, losing its sharpness, pins getting stuck, or just getting debris.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Most socket problems do not present as a clean mechanical story. They show up as electrical symptoms. A contact develops higher resistance. Heat weakens a spring</span><span data-contrast="auto">, reducing</span><span data-contrast="auto"> contact forc</span><span data-contrast="auto">e</span><span data-contrast="auto">. Debris changes the interface just enough that a device looks marginal under one test while still passing a continuity check. And because every dependent measurement sees the same impaired path, the apparent failure can move in and out of visibility without ever becoming a simple open or short.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Everything boils down to contact resistance signature,” said Jesse Ko, </span><span data-contrast="auto">C</span><span data-contrast="auto">OO of Modus Test. “Sometimes it’s not visible because it’s not </span><span data-contrast="auto">an </span><span data-contrast="auto">actual failure. It gets binned into</span><span data-contrast="auto"> a</span><span data-contrast="auto"> lower grade in frequency or voltage, so </span><span data-contrast="auto">it will</span> <span data-contrast="auto">still pass the</span><span data-contrast="auto"> test, but </span><span data-contrast="auto">it will</span> <span data-contrast="auto">be sorted into a lower bin.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">That last point is easy to understate. A bad interface doesn’t have to reject a part to do damage. It can quietly change the value assigned to good silicon, downgrading a device that belonged in a higher bin. The failure never shows up as scrap. It shows up as lost value</span><span data-contrast="auto">.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The same ambiguity appears when power delivery through the interface is compromised. A device may appear to have fewer usable cores or less frequency headroom than the silicon would deliver under a better connection. That kind of error is especially dangerous because it looks plausible. Nothing </span><span data-contrast="auto">in the result indicates that the measurement path, not the device, imposed</span><span data-contrast="auto"> the limitation.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Margins, calibration, and variance</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">This is one reason pass/fail information is becoming less sufficient as a test result. A device that passes with a wide, stable margin is not the same as one that barely passes under one set of conditions, and guard</span><span data-contrast="auto">&#8211;</span><span data-contrast="auto">bands become much harder to set when uncertainty comes </span><span data-contrast="auto">from </span><span data-contrast="auto">the </span><span data-contrast="auto">entire temporary path between instrument and device</span> <span data-contrast="auto">rather than </span><span data-contrast="auto">the instrument</span><span data-contrast="auto">.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Knowing that a device passed is less useful than knowing by how much it passed and whether that margin is stable,” said Carline. “High site counts exponentially increase the number of permutations of instrument, loadboard, socket and device interactions that need to be analyzed to thoroughly vet the system.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Calibration helps, but it doesn’t close the gap by itself. A calibration certificate captures a state at a point in time. It doesn’t guarantee that every fixture, contact, channel, and correction </span><span data-contrast="auto">remains stab</span><span data-contrast="auto">le across every marginal production measurement</span><span data-contrast="auto">.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Calibration scheduling is also diagnostic</span><span data-contrast="auto">,&#8221; said Carline. &#8220;</span>If a parametric offset tracks the tester&#8217;s calibration cycle, appearing after extended run time and disappearing after a cal routine, the instrument is implicated but not necessarily faulty. The same result can occur from operating it outside its specified capabilities.”</p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">As a diagnostic, the calibration record becomes evidence rather than a compliance artifact. However, a test cell can still </span><span data-contrast="auto">be</span> <span data-contrast="auto">within formal limits while becoming less trustworthy precisely at</span><span data-contrast="auto"> the boundary where the business decision is made.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“This is the trap</span><span data-contrast="auto">,&#8221; said </span><span data-contrast="auto">Channappa</span><span data-contrast="auto">. &#8220;</span><span data-contrast="auto">&#8216;W</span><span data-contrast="auto">ithin specification’ is a pass/fail verdict, and drift is a trend</span><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-contrast="auto">The earliest tell is usually variance, not mean.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Variance is often where the system first confesses. Retest rates begin to creep up, a marginal bin expands, two sites that used to agree begin to separate, or a specific parameter becomes less settled and more dependent on time and temperature. The </span><span data-contrast="auto">usual </span><span data-contrast="auto">reflex is to view these as product or process variation, when sometimes they are the measurement path announcing it’s become part of the problem.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The </span><span data-contrast="auto">problem</span> <span data-contrast="auto">is that production test is built for speed and throughput, while root-cause analysis is built for context. The more complex the device, the harder those demands conflict. The answer is not simply more tests or tighter limits, because both exacerbate the problem itself. The better path is to make the measurement chain visible enough that a failing result can be traced back through the site, channel, interface, thermal state, and history that produced it.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Power and heat are test conditions</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">That becomes especially clear when power and temperature are treated as part of the test condition rather than background variables. High-performance compute and AI devices can draw enormous current during test, and that current moves through a path with its own resistance, thermal behavior, and response time. When any piece of the path begins to move, the device can look like the source of a failure </span><span data-contrast="auto">that </span><span data-contrast="auto">the environment created.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“The system is becoming really complex,” said Pizza. “There is a mix between electrical performances, response time, accuracy, and also thermal control, which impacts the test results</span><span data-contrast="auto">. And if any of these elements is</span><span data-contrast="auto"> not performing correctly, </span><span data-contrast="auto">it</span><span data-contrast="auto"> can</span> <span data-contrast="auto">create false failures.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">This is about more than holding a chuck or socket at a target temperature. In high-power devices, junction temperature during test may not match the external temperature being controlled, especially when different blocks fire at different times or power delivery creates localized heating. On-die sensors help only if their data feeds back quickly enough to the handler, prober, or thermal loop. Otherwise, a device may be tested under a condition that looks controlled from outside the package but is not controlled where the failure mechanism lives.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Thermal observability inside the test cell is increasingly critical,” said Teradyne’s Carline. “The junction temperature during test can deviate significantly from the chuck or socket ambient. Integrating on-board thermal monitoring, or using tester-resident infrastructure to interrogate on-die temperature sensors, gives engineers confidence that devices are being tested at the intended thermal condition rather than an artifact of test cell thermal management.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The interface has its own version of this problem. High current through a marginal contact </span><span data-contrast="auto">generates heat, which weakens the contact, raises resistance,</span><span data-contrast="auto"> and creates still more heat. The symptom looks like a device failing under load. The better question is whether it would have failed under the intended electrical and thermal conditions.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“If you get enough IR drop there, </span><span data-contrast="auto">it will</span> <span data-contrast="auto">start self-heating, and i</span><span data-contrast="auto">t will </span><span data-contrast="auto">thermally run away and actually melt things,” said Modus Test’s Lewis. “It’s risky if your socket’s not in good condition. You’re rolling the dice with problems like that, especially if you don’t have the best protection mechanisms.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">These interactions rarely leave stable signatures. A device passes cold and fails after sustained activity. A load board</span><span data-contrast="auto">’s behavior</span><span data-contrast="auto"> shifts as components heat. A supply transient </span><span data-contrast="auto">intermittently resets a device</span><span data-contrast="auto">. That’s why the conditions swept during debug have become as important as the failing test itself, from waveform settling and sample counts to strobe timing, terminations, and stimulus margin during passing runs. The point of the sweep is to learn whether the device is failing the intended condition, or a condition the setup created.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Independent witnesses</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">Beneath all of this sits a harder truth. Electrical test is very good at identifying symptoms, but symptoms are not root causes. A resistance shift or timing failure rarely explains by itself whether the problem lives in the silicon, package, assembly, interface, or test cell</span><span data-contrast="auto">. Moreover, as packages become </span><span data-contrast="auto">increasingly</span><span data-contrast="auto"> dense</span><span data-contrast="auto">, that distinction gets harder to make from electrical data alone.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">This is where inspection and metrology become independent witnesses. X-ray inspection can reveal hidden solder defects, micro-bump voiding, or TSV anomalies. Acoustic inspection can identify delamination, cracking, and bonded-interface defects. Optical and 3D AOI capture wire-bond geometry, coplanarity, contamination, and socket or pin metrology. </span><span data-contrast="auto">N</span><span data-contrast="auto">one of them replaces electrical test, but they can show whether an electrical symptom has a physical counterpart.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Inspection provides an independent, non-destructive way to confirm whether an electrical symptom has a physical counterpart in the device under test, package, or assembly,” said Chris Rand, principal technical engineer at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/cyberoptics/">Nordson Test &amp; Inspection</a><span data-contrast="auto">. “If X-ray, acoustic, or optical inspection reveals a structural anomaly in the same location as the electrical failure, the product itself becomes the stronger suspect. If, however, the device shows no corresponding physical defect, but inspection or metrology identifies contact height variation, contamination, socket wear, misalignment, warpage, or coplanarity issues, the failure may be linked to the test interface rather than the device.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">That’s an important distinction because many physical defects do not </span><span data-contrast="auto">initially behave like hard failures</span><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-contrast="auto">Non-uniform</span> <span data-contrast="auto">wetting, micro-bump voiding, die shift, warpage, and marginal coplanarity can all produce electrical behavior that appears only under certain loading, temperature, or contact conditions. Electrical test catches the symptom. Inspection can identify the mechanism that made it intermittent</span><span data-contrast="auto"> or show that the package is physically clean, pushing</span><span data-contrast="auto"> the investigation back toward the test cell.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Some defects are not obvious as standalone inspection findings and may not create a consistent hard failure during electrical test,” said Rand. “In these cases, inspection does not simply identify a defect</span><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-contrast="auto">I</span><span data-contrast="auto">t helps explain why a marginal electrical condition appears under certain circumstances and not others. That correlation is increasingly important as packages become denser, interconnects become smaller, and failure mechanisms become more subtle.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The same logic applies further upstream. Front-end metrology can’t answer every back-end electrical question, but it can identify process variation that later </span><span data-contrast="auto">impacts</span><span data-contrast="auto"> device behavior. In silicon/silicon-germanium nanosheet stacks, for example, germanium concentration and interfacial roughness are not abstract film properties. They affect strain, carrier mobility, and ultimately performance, and by the time the electrical test sees the effect, the opportunity to correct the deposition is long gone.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;">Inspection testifies from outside the package, but a newer class of witness testifies from within it. On-die sensors measure timing margins, local voltage, and temperature at the junction while the test is running, giving engineers a reference that doesn’t depend on the health of anything between the instrument and the die.</p>
<p style="font-weight: 400;">&#8220;The package boundary has always been the limit of observability, which means power integrity issues in the load board or socket resistance shifts can look identical to a device-level problem,&#8221; said Alex Burlak, executive VP of engineering and customer success at <strong>proteanTecs</strong>. &#8220;If the on-chip voltage sensors show a stable supply rail while the tester reports a droop, the droop is in the power delivery chain outside the device. If local temperature sensors show a normal thermal gradient while the tester signals thermal stress, the thermal event did not reach the junction.&#8221;</p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Meanwhile, tiny differences in chemical concentration inside transistors can cause electrical differences. “The germanium content in the SiGe film defines the tensile strain in the silicon channels,” said Juliette van der Meer, product </span><span data-contrast="auto">marke</span><span data-contrast="auto">ting </span><span data-contrast="auto">manager for X-ray metrology at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/bruker/">Bruker</a><span data-contrast="auto">. “If the Ge concentration is outside the process spec, this will affect the strain in the channel and thus the electron mobility. The interfacial roughness also impacts electron mobility, as the electron density is highest near the surface. If that surface is rougher than it should be, then the flow is also interrupted</span><span data-contrast="auto">.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">For test engineers, that information is only as good as the handoff. A wafer that was within control limits but closer to a boundary than expected is valuable context when the same material later produces marginal electrical behavior. Without it, the test engineer sees only the late-stage symptom and has to reconstruct a history</span><span data-contrast="auto"> that</span><span data-contrast="auto"> the manufacturing flow already measured.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Front-end metrology is a key control measure to check if the future devices will be in spec,” said van der Meer. “If I were a test engineer, it would be helpful to have access to front-end results from that batch.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Data that knows what it followed</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">The difficulty is that these datasets were not built to be used together. Front-end metrology, wafer sort, package inspection, final test, retest, and field data often live in different systems with different owners. That fragmentation was tolerable when failure signatures were large and the cost of wrong conclusions was relatively low</span><span data-contrast="auto">. It’s less tolerable when </span><span data-contrast="auto">distinguishing</span><span data-contrast="auto"> a good die from a marginal die from a test-cell artifact depends on correlating data across multiple insertions and physical domains.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">This is where analytics becomes more than yield reporting. A test floor can collect enormous amounts of data and still miss the pattern if results can’t be tied to the correct site, socket, instrument, calibration state, thermal condition, or retest behavior. A retest that simply changes a bin is useful operationally. A retest that preserves device identity, original site, failing test, and recovered bin becomes diagnostic evidence.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“If I had 100 devices that didn’t pass, took them over to a different tester, ran them again, and bin 25 became bin 1 for 85% of the time, now I know I had a setup issue. It’s almost certain,” said Greg Prewitt, director of Exensio Solutions at </span><a href="https://semiengineering.com/entities/pdf-solutions/">PDF Solutions</a><span data-contrast="auto">. “If you don’t have access to some way to collect all of this data and watch the trends across tools and times and lots conveniently, you’re leaving good silicon on the table.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">That separates data volume from diagnostic value. The useful pattern is not that a</span> <span data-contrast="auto">device</span><span data-contrast="auto"> failed</span><span data-contrast="auto">, it’s that a specific failure mode changed predictably when the device was moved or remeasured, which requires an infrastructure that preserves relationships among results, equipment, locations, and time.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The baseline problem is getting harder, too. Traditional statistics assume enough observations to establish what normal looks like, but die counts per wafer are shrinking while site counts climb. The current wafer or lot may not contain </span><span data-contrast="auto">sufficient information on its own to distinguish</span><span data-contrast="auto"> product variation from test-cell variation.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“For the most part, people don’t consider context,” said Prewitt. “If we run 16 sites, I might need 50 data points per site before I start judging the devices under test. Now you need new data that’s beyond the current data set or the current test operation that you’re on.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">That’s one reason adaptive test and AI-based analytics are both promising and risky. They can identify patterns in smaller sets and see patterns humans miss, then route marginal devices to additional characterization</span><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-contrast="auto">B</span><span data-contrast="auto">ut they learn from whatever they are fed</span><span data-contrast="auto">. S</span><span data-contrast="auto">o </span><span data-contrast="auto">if a socket, channel, or thermal loop is injecting noise into the raw data, an adaptive system can mistake test-cell behavior for device behavior and build that misunderstanding into future decisions.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“If the socket variability is making more random failure noise than any other kind of </span><span data-contrast="auto">signature</span><span data-contrast="auto">, you’re not going to be able to see them,” said Lewis. “If your raw data is noisy, it doesn’t matter. You have to filter out the noise in order</span><span data-contrast="auto"> to see the real data and build the model around it.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">That caution doesn’t </span><span data-contrast="auto">necessarily </span><span data-contrast="auto">argue against </span><span data-contrast="auto">using</span><span data-contrast="auto"> AI for test analysis. </span><span data-contrast="auto">What&#8217;s needed here is</span><span data-contrast="auto"> a better foundation</span><span data-contrast="auto">. Margin logging, site tracking, calibration state, thermal data, inspection results, and front-end metrology all become more valuable when they connect to the same device or lot history</span><span data-contrast="auto"> with enough specificity that </span><span data-contrast="auto">automated systems can distinguish a real device trend from an artifact of the equipment that measured it.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Conclusion</span></b><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span><br />
<span data-contrast="auto">The broader shift is that test is moving from a screen to a source of process intelligence. It still has to protect throughput, cost, and quality, but it also has to explain why a device behaved the way it did. That matters most for known-good die and advanced packages, where a false pass destroys value later, a false fail</span><span data-contrast="auto">ure</span><span data-contrast="auto"> discards value earlier, and a false downgrade removes value without ever looking like a yield event.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Customers can no longer afford the luxury of throwing away a device larger than 10 </span><span data-contrast="auto">x</span> <span data-contrast="auto">10 centimeters</span><span data-contrast="auto"> just because one single die is not performing correctly,” said Advantest’s Pizza. “The customers now want to have more test coverage, but more distributed over the semiconductor test chain from wafer to package.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The same pressure is changing how guard</span><span data-contrast="auto">&#8211;</span><span data-contrast="auto">bands are understood. A guard</span><span data-contrast="auto">&#8211;</span><span data-contrast="auto">band is not only a buffer around a specification. It is a business decision about uncertainty, escape risk, overkill, and downstream cost. If the dominant uncertainty comes from a contactor, fixture, or thermal condition nobody is tracking, the guard</span><span data-contrast="auto">&#8211;</span><span data-contrast="auto">band may be</span><span data-contrast="auto"> hiding</span><span data-contrast="auto"> a problem that should have been fixed directly.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">“Once you have a defensible expanded uncertainty, the guard</span><span data-contrast="auto">&#8211;</span><span data-contrast="auto">band is a risk decision, not a formula,” said </span><span data-contrast="auto">Keysight’s </span><span data-contrast="auto">Ramachandra</span><span data-contrast="auto">. “Set the limit to the consequence, not to the convenience. In a chiplet era, a bad die doesn’t fail alone, it takes an expensive assembly with it.”</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The test cell lies only in a particular sense. It doesn’t invent numbers. It produces them through a temporary </span><span data-contrast="auto">electrical connection</span><span data-contrast="auto"> whose behavior may be changing in ways that are easy to miss if the result is treated as belonging only to the device. The solution is to make test accountable to the conditions that produced each result</span><span data-contrast="auto"> —</span><span data-contrast="auto"> tracking margins rather than only bins, monitoring variance rather than only averages, treating calibration as a continuous evidence chain, using inspection as a corroborating witness, and preserving enough context across insertions to </span><span data-contrast="auto">track the</span><span data-contrast="auto"> failure </span><span data-contrast="auto">path</span><span data-contrast="auto">. The number still matters, but what matters more is knowing what that number represents.</span><span data-ccp-props="{}"> </span></p>
<p><b><span data-contrast="auto">Related Articles </span></b><span data-ccp-props="{}"><br />
<a href="https://semiengineering.com/smart-test-collides-with-the-data-chain"><span data-contrast="none">Smart Test Collides With The Data Chain<br />
</span></a><span data-contrast="none">Increasing complexity is limiting the ability of machine learning models to effectively utilize test data.</span></span></p>
<p><a href="https://semiengineering.com/whats-failing-at-the-interface/"><span data-contrast="none">What’s Failing At The Interface<br />
</span></a><span data-contrast="none">In advanced packages, the interface is where problems show up, but rarely where they begin.</span></p>
<p><a href="https://semiengineering.com/adaptive-test-gaining-ground-for-hpc-and-ai-chips/"><span data-contrast="none">Adaptive Test Gaining Ground For HPC And AI Chips<br />
</span></a><span data-contrast="none">Real-time optimization is moving to the tester, but results are mixed so far.</span></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/when-the-test-cell-lies/">When The Test Cell Lies</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>

		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Jeanine Norlin]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Tue, 07 Jul 2026 07:01:17 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Blogs - PTES]]></category>
