					<description><![CDATA[<p>The role of precision dispensing and fluid management in front and back-end processes.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/microchip-manufacturing-understanding-the-semiconductor-manufacturing-process/">Microchip Manufacturing: Understanding The Semiconductor Manufacturing Process</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>Over the past several decades, the semiconductor industry has experienced sustained growth. Early expansion was driven by the widespread adoption of personal computers, while today demand is fueled by mobile devices, cloud computing, electric vehicles, and advanced connectivity.</p>
<p>As these technologies evolve, semiconductor manufacturing must support higher performance, greater device density, and increased efficiency. This continued demand places greater emphasis on the semiconductor fabrication process, where precision and process control are essential to achieving consistent results.</p>
<p>Industry forecasts point to continued expansion as technologies such as artificial intelligence, quantum computing, and 5G networks advance, further increasing the complexity of semiconductor design and manufacturing processes.</p>
<h2>Key stages in the semiconductor manufacturing process</h2>
<p>The semiconductor manufacturing process, also commonly referred to as the chip manufacturing process, involves a series of highly controlled and interconnected stages that transform raw silicon into fully functional microchips. While the specific steps can vary depending on the application, the overall process can be broadly grouped into several key phases.</p>
<p>At a high level, the process begins with wafer fabrication, where layers of materials are deposited, patterned, and etched to form integrated circuits on a silicon wafer. These steps are repeated multiple times to build complex, multi-layered structures with nanometer-level precision.</p>
<p>Following fabrication, the process moves into interconnect formation and finishing, where the intricate wiring that connects components is created and refined. As device architectures become more advanced, these interconnect layers play an increasingly critical role in overall performance and reliability.</p>
<p>Finally, wafers enter back-end processes, including dicing, assembly, and advanced packaging, where individual chips are prepared for integration into electronic devices. Inspection and testing are conducted throughout each stage to ensure quality, consistency, and yield.</p>
<p>Understanding how these stages interact is essential, as improvements in one part of the process often depend on precision and control across the entire manufacturing workflow.</p>
<h2>Role in microchip manufacturing and semiconductor fabrication</h2>
<p>For more than 15 years, <a href="https://www.nordson.com/en/about-us">Nordson</a> has supported high-quality semiconductor manufacturing, particularly in processes centered around multi-layer interconnects within the wafers used in microchip production.</p>
<p>The semiconductor manufacturing process includes both front-end and back-end stages. In the front-end, a blank silicon wafer undergoes hundreds of highly controlled steps in the wafer fabrication process to form integrated circuits, known as dies. Nordson’s <a href="https://www.nordson.com/en/divisions/electronics-solutions/your-process/industries/semiconductor-packaging">semiconductor manufacturing</a> equipment contributes to front-end finishing processes, where precision is critical to ensuring the integrity of these structures.</p>
<p>Interconnects form the intricate wiring that connects billions of components, such as transistors and capacitors, on a chip. These layers are created through the semiconductor interconnect process, which becomes increasingly complex as devices become smaller and more densely integrated. Additional interconnect layers are required, increasing both complexity and the need for precise alignment and material control.</p>
<p>Semiconductor fabrication involves repeating many of these steps multiple times to build layered structures with nanometer-level precision. As device architectures become more advanced, maintaining consistency across these layers becomes increasingly challenging.</p>
<h2>Precision in the semiconductor fabrication process</h2>
<p>Advanced semiconductor manufacturing technologies incorporate dielectric films that play a critical role in the semiconductor coating process. When combined with controlled UV curing processes, these materials help improve film integrity and durability. <a href="https://www.nordson.com/en/divisions/polymer-processing-systems/extrusion-and-coextrusion-dies---product-category-page">Slot die</a> and <a href="https://www.nordson.com/en/divisions/polymer-processing-systems/fluid-coating-systems---product-category-page">fluid coating systems</a> are used to enhance semiconductor production by enabling precise material application within the semiconductor coating process. These capabilities include technologies available through <a href="https://www.nordson.com/en/divisions/polymer-processing-systems">Nordson Polymer Processing Systems</a>.</p>
<p>Nordson’s <a href="https://www.nordson.com/en/divisions/industrial-coating-solutions">UV curing technologies</a> support semiconductor material processing applications by improving processing speed, reducing energy requirements, and enhancing process consistency. By enabling precise energy control during layered construction, these technologies contribute to more consistent outcomes in semiconductor fabrication.</p>
<p>These types of process improvements are essential as manufacturers work to balance performance, efficiency, and reliability across increasingly complex semiconductor designs.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/microchip-manufacturing-understanding-the-semiconductor-manufacturing-process/">Microchip Manufacturing: Understanding The Semiconductor Manufacturing Process</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>

		<item>
		<title>Unlocking Scalable SRG Waveguides for Mass‑Market AR/MR Displays</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/unlocking-scalable-srg-waveguides-for-mass-market-ar-mr-displays/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/unlocking-scalable-srg-waveguides-for-mass-market-ar-mr-displays/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Onto Innovation]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Tue, 07 Jul 2026 07:01:08 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Test, Measurement and Analytics]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Whitepapers]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[AR/MR displays]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[augmented reality]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[mixed reality]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Onto Innovation]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[photonics]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[SRG waveguides]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[surface relief grating]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278448</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>An approach for full process control for the HVM of SRG waveguides, encompassing metal film metrology, OCD measurements of grating parameters, overlay alignment verification, and defect inspection.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/unlocking-scalable-srg-waveguides-for-mass-market-ar-mr-displays/">Unlocking Scalable SRG Waveguides for Mass‑Market AR/MR Displays</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<p>From consumer products to industrial applications, augmented reality (AR)/mixed reality (MR) technology is one of the most innovative interactive technologies on the market today. By overlaying digital information onto the physical world, AR/MR technology improves how people see, understand, and interact with their environment in real time. The industrial applications alone are cause for celebration, whether this technology is being used for training, assembly, or troubleshooting.</p>
<p>Be that as it may, one of the most significant challenges facing AR/MR lies in manufacturing the photonic components that control how light is generated, diffracted, guided, and delivered to the human eye.</p>
<p>An AR display usually consists of a light engine and an optical combiner. The light engine serves as a display image source, while the combiner delivers the displayed images to the viewer’s eye and transmits environment light (Figure 1). Surface relief grating (SRG) waveguides play a role in these optical combiners by coupling display light into, expanding it within, and diffracting it out of a transparent substrate toward the eye of the intended viewer. This component must deliver precise diffraction behavior while remaining transparent, lightweight, and scalable for high-volume manufacturing (HVM).</p>
<p>Balancing optical performance and manufacturability, however, places significant demands on fabrication and process control. Faced with this obstacle, device makers require solutions capable of addressing any negative impacts to launch timelines, unit costs, and OEM qualification.</p>
<p>Read <a href="https://live-ontoinnovation-com.pantheonsite.io/resources/unlocking-scalable-srg-waveguides-for-mass-market-ar-mr-displays/">more here</a>.<br />
<img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="alignnone  wp-image-24278450" src="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-06-at-10.14.03-AM-1.png?resize=470%2C213&#038;ssl=1" alt="" width="470" height="213" srcset="https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-06-at-10.14.03-AM-1.png?resize=300%2C136&amp;ssl=1 300w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-06-at-10.14.03-AM-1.png?resize=1024%2C465&amp;ssl=1 1024w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-06-at-10.14.03-AM-1.png?resize=768%2C349&amp;ssl=1 768w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-06-at-10.14.03-AM-1.png?resize=1536%2C697&amp;ssl=1 1536w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-06-at-10.14.03-AM-1.png?resize=600%2C272&amp;ssl=1 600w, https://i0.wp.com/semiengineering.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Screenshot-2026-07-06-at-10.14.03-AM-1.png?w=1590&amp;ssl=1 1590w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 470px) 100vw, 470px" /></p>
<p><strong>Fig.1: Diagram of SRG waveguide-based AR display. Source Onto Innovation</strong></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>

		<post-id xmlns="com-wordpress:feed-additions:1">24278448</post-id>	</item>
		<item>
		<title>Research Bits: July 6</title>
		<link>https://semiengineering.com/research-bits-july-6/</link>
					<comments>https://semiengineering.com/research-bits-july-6/#respond</comments>
		
		<dc:creator><![CDATA[Jesse Allen]]></dc:creator>
		<pubDate>Mon, 06 Jul 2026 07:01:19 +0000</pubDate>
				<category><![CDATA[Low Power-High Performance]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Research]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Research: LPHP]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[ETH Zurich]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[ferroelectrics]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Institute of Science Tokyo]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[materials]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[National Sun Yat-Sen University]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[neural networks]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[pixels]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[sensors]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[titanium dioxide]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[University of California Berkeley]]></category>
		<category><![CDATA[Yokohama City University]]></category>
		<guid isPermaLink="false">https://semiengineering.com/?p=24278426</guid>

					<description><![CDATA[<p>Neural net predicts semiconductor properties; ferroelectric titanium dioxide thin film; bidirectional pixels.</p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/research-bits-july-6/">Research Bits: July 6</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></description>
										<content:encoded><![CDATA[<h2>Neural net predicts semiconductor properties</h2>
<p>Researchers from the Institute of Science Tokyo, Yokohama City University, and National Sun Yat-sen University devised a <a href="https://www.isct.ac.jp/en/news/tky57fxj4rub">tandem neural network</a> capable of quickly inferring key physical parameters of semiconductor materials from simple transistor measurements.</p>
<p>The approach uses two machine learning models linked in series. The first model tries to estimate material properties from transistor measurements. Then, a pre-trained forward network that uses the material estimates produced by the first model to reconstruct the original transistor characteristics. By using the output of this second model as part of the training input given to the first model, the overall system learns to find solutions that are both mathematically plausible and physically consistent.</p>
<p>The tandem neural network (TNN) was trained using 1,000 amorphous indium–gallium–zinc oxide (a-IGZO) transistor datasets, covering six physical parameters including defect densities, trap-state characteristics, and electron mobility. The TNN was able to infer all six parameters from a single current–voltage curve in under one millisecond with high accuracy.</p>
<p>In tests using real transistors fabricated in the lab under different conditions, the model was able to reproduce their measured behavior without any additional optimization steps or adjustments.</p>
<p>&#8220;Compared with conventional device-simulation-based methods requiring hundreds of iterative calculations and taking tens of hours to several days, the proposed approach achieved a speedup of over six orders of magnitude,&#8221; said Keisuke Ide, an associate professor at Institute of Science Tokyo, in a press release. &#8220;We expect our approach to be applicable not only to semiconductors but also to a wide variety of inverse problems involving multivaluedness.&#8221; [1]</p>
<h2>Ferroelectric titanium dioxide thin film</h2>
<p>Researchers from the University of California Berkeley, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, and SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory transformed <a href="https://engineering.berkeley.edu/news/2026/05/researchers-discover-a-new-pathway-to-building-energy-efficient-computing-chips/">titanium dioxide</a> (TiO₂) into a ferroelectric material by reducing its thickness to less than 3nm.</p>
<p>“We were quite surprised when we discovered that as the thickness of TiO₂ films dropped below 3nm, the material becomes ferroelectric, a phase in which it shows spontaneous electric polarization that can be switched by applying an electric field,” said Sayeef Salahuddin, professor of electrical engineering and computer sciences and of materials science and engineering at UC Berkeley and a senior faculty scientist at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, in a press release. “More importantly, this new ferroelectric behavior remains stable in films as thin as about 1nm, approximately two unit-cells thick.” Salahuddin suggested that other common dielectric materials of this class might develop new electronic behaviors in atomic-scale dimensions.</p>
<p>The TiO₂ films retained their ferroelectric properties when deposited on different substrates. “Our work demonstrates that this ferroelectricity is stable on both crystalline, or silicon, and amorphous carbon film substrates. This indicates the feasibility of integration with silicon-based technologies and beyond,” said Koushik Das, a graduate researcher in the College of Chemistry and the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences at UC Berkeley, in a press release. “The ultrathin TiO₂ films can be grown at a low temperature, less than 400°C, using atomic layer deposition (ALD), a technique already used in state-of the-art chip fabrication. Furthermore, we can produce thin films with uniform thickness across all surfaces and polarization properties conducive to enabling new functionality for 3D integrated electronics.” [2]</p>
<h2>Bidirectional pixels</h2>
<p>Researchers at ETH Zurich developed <a href="https://ethz.ch/en/news-and-events/eth-news/news/2026/06/a-new-type-of-pixel.html">bidirectional pixels</a> that can both steer light and analyze it, an advance the team hopes could lead to devices that function as a camera and a display at the same time.</p>
<p>The pixel uses wave-shaped sculpted surfaces that can control and analyze the light intensity, oscillation phase, and polarization. For steering, the pixel transforms the incoming light into a surface wave propagating along the surface of the chip. At a different position within the pixel, the surface wave is scattered back out of the material as a light wave. Through interference of the light waves, patterns and images can be created. What these images will look like and what kind of surface pattern is needed for a specific image can be calculated using Fourier analysis.</p>
<p>“We can also, however, apply the principle of interference and Fourier analysis in the opposite direction to analyze light using the Fourier pixel”, said Sander Vonk, a postdoctoral researcher at ETH Zurich, in a statement. “Thanks to the fact that the relevant surface profiles of the pixels can be determined using Fourier analysis, we can combine the control and analysis of amplitude, phase, and polarization on a single pixel.”</p>
<p>The oscillation phase of the light can be made visible by superimposing the light wave and a reference wave on the pixel. The researchers then capture the interference pattern of the scattered light from both waves with a camera. The pattern can be used to calculate the phase of the light. A similar method is used to analyze the light&#8217;s polarization state.</p>
<p>Next, the team plans to create a matrix made of many Fourier pixels to create more complex devices. [3]</p>
<h3>References</h3>
<p>[1] M. Kimura, K. Ide, K.-J. Zhou, et al. Tandem Neural Network Rapidly Solves Multivalued Inverse Problems: Application to Oxide-Semiconductor Characterization. Advanced Intelligent Systems. <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/aisy.70437">https://doi.org/10.1002/aisy.70437</a></p>
<p>[2] K. Das, K. Reidy, S. Husain, et al. Ferroelectricity in atomic-scale titanium dioxide dielectric films. Science 392, 280-284 (2026). <a href="https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aec9417">https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aec9417</a></p>
<p>[3] Y.M. Glauser, S.J.W. Vonk, D.B. Seda, et al. Fourier pixels for bidirectional light control. Nature 655, 87–92 (2026). <a href="https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-026-10681-7">https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-026-10681-7</a></p>
<p>The post <a href="https://semiengineering.com/research-bits-july-6/">Research Bits: July 6</a> appeared first on <a href="https://semiengineering.com">Semiconductor Engineering</a>.</p>
]]></content:encoded>
